title: UMG’s Michael Nash sees ‘no audience’ for explosion in AI music - Music Ally
text:
Universal Music Group’s digital chief Michael Nash sat down with Splice CEO Kakul Srivastava at last week’s HumanX AI conference, offering the latest insight into how both companies see the AI-music space.
Nash went in hard on “the false narrative of artist replacement” by AI, suggesting that it was rooted in a “deeply flawed investment thesis” that “AI eliminates music-content supply constraints, kills legacy model, jailbreaks music economy”.
“The problem with that thesis is that an infinity of music already exists. There were no music content supply constraints prior to the rise of AI,” said Nash.
“A nuclear explosion in production volume of content through AI doesn’t have a market. There’s no audience for that. It’s not addressing any kind of need.”
The emphasis of the session, moderated by Trapital’s Dan Runcie, was on the potential of AI tools for human artists. Srivastava was optimistic that this is the direction of travel for the wider market.
“I think the next generation of AI companies are fundamentally trying to do the right thing, where it comes to respecting artist rights and copyright and legal framework,” she said.
“It’s not rocket science, and you really are seeing that the early days were different. It was a little bit Wild West, but we are one of many companies that are trying to do the right thing.”

Stuart Dredge
Music Ally's Head of Insight
